evals.name: access-control-in-go
evals.expected_output: Finds: A01 IDOR on GetOrder (no ownership check; any authenticated user can fetch any order). A01 / API5 broken function-level authorization on DeleteUser (only checks any authenticated user, not admin role; anyone can delete any user). A03/API3 improper property-level exposure (internal_notes returned to client). Provides Go-idiomatic fixes (ownership clause in query, role-check middleware, explicit column list in SELECT). Cites Top 10 + API Top 10 correctly. Severity: critical on DeleteUser, high on GetOrder, medium on property exposure.

evals.name: fastapi-jwt-mass-assignment
evals.prompt:
Give this FastAPI module a security audit. It's the user-profile service; auth and JWT are handled here.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import jwt

app = FastAPI()
SECRET = "dev-secret-change-me"

class UserOut(BaseModel):
    id: int
    name: str
    email: str
    password_hash: str
    role: str
    api_token: str
    internal_notes: str | None = None

@app.get("/users/{user_id}", response_model=UserOut)
async def get_user(user_id: int, authorization: str = Header(default="")):
    token = authorization.split(" ", 1)[1] if " " in authorization else ""
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, options={"verify_signature": False})
    except Exception:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="bad token")
    user = db.find_user(user_id)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404)
    return user

@app.patch("/users/{user_id}")
async def update_user(user_id: int, updates: dict, authorization: str = Header(default="")):
    token = authorization.split(" ", 1)[1] if " " in authorization else ""
    jwt.decode(token, options={"verify_signature": False})
    db.update_user(user_id, **updates)
    return {"ok": True}
```
evals.expected_output: Finds: API2 broken authentication — jwt.decode with verify_signature=False; tokens never validated (critical). API1 BOLA on GET — no caller-vs-target ownership check. API3 broken property-level authorization on response — returns password_hash, api_token, role, internal_notes. API3 mass assignment on PATCH — accepts arbitrary dict so caller can set role, password_hash, api_token. A02 hardcoded secret 'dev-secret-change-me' in source. Provides fixes: jwt.decode with algorithms pin and secret from env, explicit response schema / field filter, PATCH schema limited to name/email, ownership check or admin role. Cites correct codes (API2:2023, API1:2023, API3:2023, Top 10 A02).

evals.name: mixed-infra-hardening
evals.prompt:
Please review these three infra fragments from the same service and produce a consolidated hardening plan. They're for a customer-facing payments API.

**Dockerfile:**
```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:latest
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y python3 python3-pip curl netcat
COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN pip3 install -r requirements.txt
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["python3", "app.py"]
```

**Kubernetes Deployment (excerpt):**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: acme/payments:latest
        ports: [{containerPort: 8080}]
        env:
        - name: DB_URL
          value: "postgres://paydb:hunter2@db.prod.internal:5432/payments"
        - name: STRIPE_KEY
          value: "sk_live_51Hn2kLp..."
```

**Nginx reverse proxy (excerpt):**
```nginx
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name api.example.com;
    server_tokens on;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://payments-service:8080;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    }
}
```
evals.expected_output: Routes to Top 10 + K8s references. Finds across the three files: [Dockerfile] base image :latest and unpinned, no USER directive so container runs as root, unneeded tools installed (curl, netcat) expanding lateral-movement surface, no HEALTHCHECK, pip install as root; [K8s] K01 missing securityContext entirely (runAsNonRoot, drop capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem), K01 no resource limits/requests, K01 image :latest, K08 secrets in plaintext env (DB password, Stripe live key — also A02 on Top 10 side), suggest moving to Secret + CSI driver or external store; [Nginx] A05 listen 80 with no TLS / HTTPS redirect, server_tokens on leaks version, missing security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Referrer-Policy), no rate limiting. Consolidates into prioritized report: Stripe live key in manifest is critical (key rotation needed immediately), privileged root container + plaintext DB password high, image:latest and missing headers medium, installed tools low.

evals.name: typescript-llm-agent
evals.expected_output: Uses ONLY real OWASP codes (LLM01–LLM10, ASI01–ASI10) — never the fabricated AG## codes. Finds: LLM01 prompt injection (string-concatenated prompt, user history interleaved with system); LLM07 system prompt leakage (admin passphrase sits in the system prompt and is therefore exfiltratable via prompt-injection or model coaxing — note that prompt-based auth is itself insecure regardless of leakage); LLM05 improper output handling (regex parses the model's output and invokes a JS function with raw args — equivalent to RCE on the underlying tools); LLM06 excessive agency (delete_account and send_email both reachable from any user session without scoping to caller identity); LLM10 unbounded consumption (max_tokens_to_sample 4000 per turn, no rate limit, no iteration cap, no spend budget); ASI02 tool misuse (no argument validation or allowlisting on tool args); ASI03 identity & privilege abuse (no end-user identity is threaded to the tools; the agent acts on the process-level credentials). Provides fixes: role-separated messages (system + user messages via the Messages API, not a single concatenated prompt), move authorization out of the prompt and into a policy layer, replace the ACTION: regex with a structured tool-use protocol validated by a schema, scope tools to caller identity, add per-user rate and spend limits. Bonus if it flags that 'claude-2' is an outdated model ID but does not treat it as the primary finding.

evals.name: rails-multi-issue-controller
evals.prompt:
Security review on this Rails controller — an internal tool for the ops team, but it ingests from third parties. Please catch anything ship-stopping.

```ruby
class OrdersController < ApplicationController
  before_action :require_login

  # GET /orders?customer_id=123
  def index
    @orders = Order.where("customer_id = #{params[:customer_id]}").to_a
    render json: @orders
  end

  # POST /orders
  def create
    @order = Order.new(params[:order])
    @order.save!
    render json: @order
  end

  # POST /orders/import  body: {source_url: "..."}
  def import
    url = params[:source_url]
    data = open(url).read
    records = JSON.parse(data)
    @orders = records.map { |attrs| Order.create!(attrs) }
    render json: { imported: @orders.size }
  end
end
```
evals.expected_output: Finds multiple orthogonal issues in ~30 lines: A03 SQL injection in #index — string-interpolated customer_id into a where clause (use parameterized: Order.where(customer_id: params[:customer_id])); A03/API3 mass assignment in #create and #import — Order.new / Order.create!(params[:order]) / Order.create!(attrs) with whole params hash or attacker-supplied third-party data — use strong parameters (params.require(:order).permit(:product, :total, :customer_id)); A10/API7 SSRF + RCE in #import — Kernel#open with a user-controlled URL permits internal-network access AND Ruby's open() with a '|command' prefix can execute shell commands (use URI.open or a vetted HTTP client with scheme allowlist + private-IP rejection); A01 also missing — require_login ensures any authenticated ops user can read or write any customer_id's orders; API10 / A08 unsafe consumption of external API in #import (no schema validation, no timeout, TLS not enforced by default in open()). Provides concrete fixes with strong parameters, parameterized query, URI parsing + allowlist, and suggests replacing open() with a typed HTTP client like Faraday or httpx with schema validation.
